comment.body:
Thanks, you were right. The first test was weak because it manually moved the filtering reader to the new file but did not preserve the stale canonical scroll state from the panic.

I replaced it in b214d91 with `TestIssue426SwitchingToSmallerFileClearsStaleScrollPosition`. I verified it against `origin/master` by applying only the new test there; without the file-switching change it fails with:

`panic: scrollPosition index={70} not found in allLines size 48, indexed {0}-{47}`

On this branch the same test passes, along with:

- `go test ./internal -run 'TestIssue426SwitchingToSmallerFileClearsStaleScrollPosition|TestShortenedInput|TestIssue415Panic|TestIssue399' -count=1`
- `go test ./...`

issue.title: Support custom SSH usernames for install sources
issue.body:
Implemented parsing for SSH source URLs that use a custom username, such as GitHub Enterprise Data Residency remotes like `acme@acme.ghe.com:Org/repo.git`. The parser now preserves the SSH username in the clone URL, skips GitHub shorthand expansion for these inputs, and normalizes custom-username SSH URLs for conflict detection.

This fixes installs from GHE Data Residency environments where the SSH username is not `git`.

Fixes #176

Tests:
- `go test ./internal/install -run 'TestParseSource_GitSSH|TestParseSource_GitHubEnterprise|TestParseSource_GitHubEnterprise_TrackName|TestNormalizeCloneURL'`
- `./scripts/test.sh --unit --quiet`
- `./scripts/test.sh --quiet`

performed_via_github_app.description: Bring ChatGPT and Codex to your GitHub repositories.

comment.body:
Checked this path locally because it touches the install source parser and the PR is currently behind main. I did not find a blocker.

What I verified:
- custom SSH usernames keep the intended clone URL while conflict normalization strips the SSH username for repo identity matching
- `*.ghe.com` now flows through platform detection, `GitHubOwner` / `GitHubRepo`, and GitHub API base selection
- `git merge-tree 549d466f8f1b4903e45cae2816916e80c8601c65 HEAD origin/main` did not report a textual conflict against current `origin/main`

Local validation on `5fd487b8`:

- `go test ./internal/install -count=1`

The only operational note I see is that the branch is behind main, but the install-package changes looked clean in the merge check above.

issue.title: Preserved project links during adopt resync
issue.body:
## Summary
- Passed the project root through the shared adopt apply path before re-syncing targets.
- Kept project-mode `symlink` and `merge` target re-sync on the existing relative-link behavior instead of falling back to absolute links.
- Updated the project adopt integration assertion to resolve symlink targets across macOS `/private/var` canonicalization and to catch absolute project links on non-Windows platforms.

## Context
Follow-up to #181. The failing macOS integration job showed `TestAdoptProject_MigratesAndResyncs` comparing a canonical `/private/var/...` symlink target against the logical temp path. While checking that, I found the re-sync path was passing an empty project root, so project-mode adopt created absolute links instead of the portable relative links used by normal project sync.

## Tests
- `go build -o bin/skillshare ./cmd/skillshare`
- `SKILLSHARE_TEST_BINARY=$PWD/bin/skillshare go test -v -race ./tests/integration -run '^TestAdoptProject_MigratesAndResyncs$' -count=1`
- `go test ./internal/adopt ./cmd/skillshare -run 'TestApply_ReSyncHonorsCopyMode|TestAdopt' -count=1`
- `make test-unit`

comment.body:

issue.title: panic on switching from large file to next file that is smaller
issue.body:
I am viewing multiple files. One is large, and I scroll to the end. Then I type :n to get to next file which is shorter, and I get a panic. I don't get a panic when I scroll back to the top before switching to the next file.

moor version v2.13.3

```
Panic recovery timestamp: 2026-05-30 23:22:49.401347028 +0200 CEST m=+15.791056049

panic: scrollPosition index={70} not found in allLines size 48, indexed {70}-{117}:
        :
        (internal.scrollPosition) {
         internalDontTouch: (internal.scrollPositionInternal) {
          lineIndex: (*linemetadata.Index)(0x2d7430e24830)({
           index: (int) 60
          }),
          delta: (linemetadata.ScreenLines) 0,
          name: (string) (len=14) "Pager Makefile",
          canonicalizing: (bool) false,
          canonical: (internal.scrollPositionCanonical) {
           width: (int) 190,
           height: (linemetadata.ScreenLines) 48,
           showLineNumbers: (bool) true,
           showStatusBar: (bool) true,
           wrapLongLines: (bool) false,
           pagerLineCount: (int) 108,
           lineIndex: (*linemetadata.Index)(0x2d7430e24830)(<already shown>),
           deltaScreenLines: (linemetadata.ScreenLines) 0
          }
         }
        }
         [recovered, repanicked]

goroutine 1 [running]:
main.main.func1()
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/cmd/moor/moor.go:691 +0x1d4
panic({0x6b2880?, 0x2d743127c2d0?})
        runtime/panic.go:860 +0x13a
main.startPaging.func1()
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/cmd/moor/moor.go:754 +0xb8
panic({0x6b2880?, 0x2d743127c2d0?})
        runtime/panic.go:860 +0x13a
github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal.(*Pager).internalRenderLines(0x2d7430f2a248, 0x1)
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal/screenLines.go:171 +0xadc
github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal.(*Pager).renderLines(0x2d7430f2a248)
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal/screenLines.go:88 +0x54
github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal.(*Pager).redraw(0x2d7430f2a248, {0x0, 0x0})
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal/screenLines.go:50 +0xa8
github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal.(*Pager).StartPaging(0x2d7430f2a248, {0x956c10, 0x2d7430f29560}, 0x2d7430f79ec0, 0x2d74311e1de0)
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/internal/pager.go:638 +0x32c
main.startPaging(0x0?, {0x956c10?, 0x2d7430f29560?}, 0x9?, 0x9?)
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/cmd/moor/moor.go:762 +0x53
main.main()
        github.com/walles/moor/v2/cmd/moor/moor.go:718 +0x267
```

comment.body:
Thanks, this trace lines up with the stale scroll position path fixed in #428.

The important part is that after `:n`, the pager switches to the next reader, but rendering still tries to resolve the previous file's scroll position against the new file's rendered window. In the trace, the panic happens from `internalRenderLines()` immediately after `Switched to next file`, with a saved scroll position that no longer exists for the new file.

#428 resets the pager scroll position when switching files via `:n`, `:p`, or `:x`, and adds a regression test that switches from a larger file scrolled near the end to a smaller file.

Validation from the PR branch:

```bash
go test ./internal -run 'TestSwitchingToSmallerFileResetsScrollPosition|TestShortenedInput|TestIssue415Panic'
go test ./...
```

issue.title: Is it possible to add support for mcp tools?
issue.body: Editing each agent config files is quit a burden LoL

comment.body:
I did a quick pass over the current config/extras flow to see where MCP could fit without violating the architecture concern from #30.

Current relevant shape:
- `ExtraConfig` / `ExtraTargetConfig` already model arbitrary resource groups, but targets are directories (`path`, `mode`, `flatten`, optional `extension`).
- `sync.SyncExtra()` syncs files into a target directory. With `extension`, it still transforms one source file into one output file under that target directory.
- `TargetConfig` has first-class `skills` and `agents` subkeys, but no generic structured-config resource type.

That means MCP support probably should not start as "sync extras into each agent config file". That would require a new JSON merge/apply engine, conflict policy, backup/rollback behavior, and per-tool schemas, which is the large maintenance surface called out in #30.

A narrower non-destructive path that seems compatible with the current architecture:
1. Treat MCP servers as portable source definitions, e.g. one file per server under an `extras/mcp/`-style source directory.
2. Add a render/preview layer first, not an apply layer: generate target-specific config snippets to stdout or a generated sidecar directory, but do not mutate the real tool config files.
3. Keep target adapters explicit and small. Start with one target schema only, with tests for preserving env/args and rejecting machine-specific absolute paths unless explicitly allowed.
4. Only after that shape is accepted, consider an opt-in `apply` mode with `--dry-run` default, backups, and clear conflict errors when the existing JSON already has the same server key.

So the first PR could avoid the hard part entirely: parser + renderer + docs/tests for one MCP target, no writes to live config files. That would answer the "editing each config is a burden" use case partially, while keeping the destructive JSON merge problem out of scope until the schema and UX are proven.

issue.title: Separate `TrustedBuilders` from `SuggestedBuilders`

labels.name: user-35446a48/repo-b1c5fb21
labels.description: Issue that requests a new feature or improvement.

issue.body:
## Summary

_Note: This draft PR builds on unmerged changes introduced in #2573 - see https://github.com/runesoerensen/pack/compare/runesoerensen/consolidate-trusted-builder-logic...runesoerensen:pack:runesoerensen/separate-trusted-suggested-builders to inspect only the commits introduced since that branch._

Separate trust decisions from display metadata by splitting `KnownBuilder` into dedicated data structures for each concern.

- Replace `KnownBuilder` struct with separate `TrustedBuilders` (flat `[]string` for trust decisions) and `SuggestedBuilder` / `SuggestedBuilders` (display metadata for `builder suggest`)
- Add invariant test ensuring every suggested builder is also a trusted builder
- Remove `Trusted` and `Suggested` boolean fields that previously required filtering

## Output

N/A - no user-facing output changes.

## Documentation

- Should this change be documented?
    - [x] No

## Related
Resolves #2574

comment.body:
I took a look at this draft while checking the #2574 / #2572 trust split path. One edge to keep before this leaves draft: after the split, `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()` is exact-match again (`paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest` is expected to return false in the current test), but `pkg/client/build.go` still defaults `opts.TrustBuilder = builder.IsKnownTrustedBuilder`.

That means the command path is covered by `IsTrustedBuilder()`, but the client default build path can still miss tag variants of tagless trusted builders. That seems to preserve part of the #2572 inconsistency after the refactor.

A narrow fix would be either:
- keep `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()` reference-aware for entries in `TrustedBuilders`, or
- add a separate reference-aware helper for the default client trust checker and use that from `pkg/client/build.go`.

Suggested regression coverage: a client-level build test where `BuildOptions.Builder` is `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest` and `TrustBuilder` is left nil, asserting the default trusted-builder path is used.

I ran these against the draft branch:
- `go test ./internal/builder -run TestTrustedBuilder -count=1`
- `go test ./pkg/client -run 'TestBuild' -count=1`

Both pass currently, so this looks like a coverage gap rather than an existing failing test.

issue.title: Add command/prompt sync presets for Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex
issue.body:
## Summary

Add first-class support or presets for syncing reusable slash-command prompt files across tools that already support this workflow:

- Cursor: Markdown commands under `.cursor/commands/` (and commonly user-level `~/.cursor/commands/`) become `/command-name` entries.
- Claude Code: existing `.claude/commands/*.md` and `~/.claude/commands/*.md` files still work; current Claude Code docs say custom commands have been merged into skills, and commands keep working with the same frontmatter. Skills are the preferred richer format, but command files remain a compatibility target.
- Codex CLI / IDE extension: custom prompts are deprecated in favor of skills, but still supported as Markdown prompt files under `~/.codex/prompts/`, invoked as `/prompts:<name>`. Codex scans top-level Markdown files in that folder.

Today this is technically possible with `extras`, but users have to know every target path and each tool's exact behavior. A built-in command/prompt preset would make this much less error-prone.

## Why this matters

A common workflow is a small command such as `commit-smart.md`:

- inspect recent commit style
- inspect staged diff (or unstaged diff if nothing is staged)
- generate a commit message
- run `git commit` with that message

This is exactly the kind of lightweight workflow that users want available as `/commit-smart` in Cursor and Claude Code, and as `/prompts:commit-smart` in Codex. It is too small to require a full skill directory in every tool, but valuable enough to sync across machines and agents.

## Current skillshare behavior

`extras` already supports arbitrary resources, for example:

```yaml
extras:
  - name: commands
    targets:
      - path: ~/.claude/commands
      - path: ~/.cursor/commands
      - path: ~/.codex/prompts
```

This works as a low-level escape hatch, but it has several rough edges:

1. Users must manually know that Codex calls this `prompts`, not `commands`, and invokes it as `/prompts:<name>`.
2. Users must know that Codex custom prompts are deprecated and global-only, while Codex skills are preferred for shared/repo workflows.
3. Users must know that Claude Code command files still work, but skills are now the recommended richer path.
4. Future tool-specific conversion problems are easy to miss. Issue #136 already shows this for Gemini, where Markdown command extras need to become TOML.

## Proposal

Add one of these product surfaces:

### Option A: first-class `commands` resource

Support commands similarly to skills/agents, with target-aware paths and documentation:

```bash
skillshare commands init --targets claude,cursor,codex
skillshare commands sync
skillshare commands collect --from cursor
skillshare commands list --json
```

Target mapping could start conservative:

- `claude`: `~/.claude/commands` globally, `.claude/commands` in project mode
- `cursor`: `.cursor/commands` in project mode, and optionally `~/.cursor/commands` globally if the project wants to support the user-level path
- `codex`: `~/.codex/prompts` globally; document that Codex invokes these as `/prompts:<name>` and that skills are preferred for repo-shared workflows

### Option B: extras presets / target aliases

Keep `extras` as the implementation, but let users specify tool targets instead of raw paths:

```bash
skillshare extras init commands --target claude --target cursor --target codex
```

This would expand to the right target paths and attach target-specific notes/warnings. Later, the same mechanism could support `format: toml` or similar for Gemini command conversion (#136).

## Acceptance criteria

- A user can sync one Markdown command source to Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex without manually remembering all three path conventions.
- Docs explain the invocation differences:
  - Cursor/Claude: `/command-name`
  - Codex: `/prompts:command-name`
- Docs explain Codex's current deprecation status: custom prompts still work, but skills are recommended for reusable instructions that should be shared through a repo or invoked implicitly.
- Project vs global behavior is explicit, especially because Codex prompts are currently local Codex-home files rather than project-discovered files.
- The implementation does not break existing `extras` configs.

## Related issues

- #134 asked for slash command sync, but it was closed after assuming Codex intended to remove all slash command functionality. Current docs show the more precise state: Codex custom prompts are deprecated in favor of skills, but still supported and invokable as `/prompts:<name>`.
- #136 covers a target-specific format conversion problem for Gemini commands, which would fit naturally into the same command/prompt preset story.

comment.body:
I did a current-main pass after the recent extras extension work, and I think Option B is now the smallest first step.

Current flow as I read it:

- `cmd/skillshare/extras_init.go` treats every `--target` value as a raw path and stores it directly in `config.ExtraTargetConfig{Path: ...}` for both global and project config.
- `sync extras` already has the right lower-level surface for tool-specific output: `ExtraTargetConfig.Extension` is resolved during sync, transform targets use copy semantics, `sync --all --json` follows the same path, and `extras collect` already treats extension-managed targets as one-way.
- `extras list` already reports extension-backed target status using the extension `output_ext`, so a commands preset should not need a new sync model.
- The TUI currently suggests generic rules paths, including `~/.codex/rules`, which is probably misleading for this specific workflow because Codex command-style prompts live under `~/.codex/prompts`.

A narrow first PR could keep `extras` as the implementation and add only preset expansion for command/prompt extras:

- add a small resolver for command target presets, covered by unit tests;
- wire it into `extras init commands ...` for CLI first, leaving existing raw path configs untouched;
- map global presets conservatively:
  - `claude` -> `~/.claude/commands`
  - `cursor` -> `~/.cursor/commands`
  - `codex` -> `~/.codex/prompts`
- map project presets only where the target is project-local:
  - `claude` -> `.claude/commands`
  - `cursor` -> `.cursor/commands`
  - keep Codex project mode explicit, either by rejecting `codex` with a clear message or requiring the user to pass `~/.codex/prompts` manually, since Codex prompts are currently Codex-home files rather than project-discovered files;
- update `extras init` help/TUI placeholders so command examples mention Codex prompts and the invocation difference.

I would avoid adding `skillshare commands ...` in the first PR. It looks like the existing extras model is already capable enough; the missing piece is the target convention knowledge at init time plus clear docs/help text. If this direction matches the intended product shape, the implementation can stay small and should not affect existing extras configs or sync behavior.

issue.title: Consolidate trust logic into `IsTrustedBuilder`

labels.name: user-35446a48/repo-b1c5fb21
labels.description: Issue that requests a new feature or improvement.

issue.body:
## Summary

Consolidate all trust checking into `IsTrustedBuilder` so callers don't need to separately check `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()`, and use `name.ParseReference` for consistent reference matching across both known and user-configured builders.

- `IsTrustedBuilder` now checks both known trusted builders and user-configured trusted builders
- Uses `name.ParseReference` for consistent reference matching across both sources - entries without a tag match any tag in the repository, entries with a tag require an exact match
- Remove now-unnecessary `|| IsKnownTrustedBuilder()` calls from `build.go`, `config_trusted_builder.go`, and `builder_inspect.go`

## Output

#### Before
```
$ pack builder inspect paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest
...
Trusted: No
```

#### After
```
$ pack builder inspect paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest
...
Trusted: Yes
```

## Documentation
- Should this change be documented?
    - [ ] No

## Related
Resolves #2572

issue.title: Match known trusted builders by repository

labels.name: user-35446a48/repo-b1c5fb21
labels.description: Issue that requests a new feature or improvement.

issue.body:
## Summary

- Matched known trusted builders with the same reference-aware semantics used for configured trusted builders.
- Kept explicitly tagged known builders tag-exact, so entries like `heroku/builder:24` do not trust unrelated tags.
- Centralized command trust checks on `IsTrustedBuilder` so build, inspect, and config add paths do not patch known and configured trust separately.

## Context

Fixes #2572.

Known trusted builders listed without a tag, such as `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base`, previously only matched the exact string and did not trust `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest`. Configured trusted builders already matched tagless entries by repository, so this applies the same behavior to known trusted builders while preserving exact tag matching for tagged known entries.

## Validation

- `go test ./internal/builder -run TestTrustedBuilder -count=1`
- `go test ./internal/builder ./internal/commands -run 'TestTrustedBuilder|TestBuildCommand|TestBuilderInspectCommand|TestTrustedBuilderCommand' -count=1`
- `go test ./internal/commands ./pkg/client -count=1`

Note: `go test ./internal/builder -count=1` still fails locally on `TestBuilder/.../when_CNB_BUILD_CONFIG_DIR_is_defined/adds_the_env_vars_as_files_to_the_image` because it expects `/cnb/dup-build-config-dir/env/SOME_KEY` but the generated layer contains `/cnb/build-config/env/SOME_KEY`. The trust-builder suite in that package passes.

comment.body:
I missed #2573 when I opened this. That PR already covers the same issue and was opened earlier, so I am closing this duplicate in favor of #2573.

For reference, this branch added extra caller coverage around the same fix (`build`, `builder inspect`, and `config trusted-builders add`) plus a current-main CI run. I will leave the context on #2573 in case those tests are useful there.

payload.description: CLI for building apps using Cloud Native Buildpacks

issue.title: Trust checks are fragmented and known builders without a tag don't match tagged queries

labels.name: user-35446a48/repo-234e274a
labels.description: Issue that reports an unexpected behaviour.

labels.name: user-c64ca2e7/repo-25a2bbaf
labels.description: Issue ready to be worked on.

issue.body:
### Summary

`IsTrustedBuilder` only checks user-configured trusted builders (`cfg.TrustedBuilders`), so callers that need to also recognize known trusted builders must separately call `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()`. This split makes it easy for trust checks to diverge across commands (see #2570).

Additionally, `IsKnownTrustedBuilder` uses exact string matching, so known builders listed without a tag (e.g. `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base`) don't match when a specific tag is used (e.g. `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest`). `IsTrustedBuilder` already supports matching by repository for user-configured builders (via `name.ParseReference`), but this is not applied to known builders.

Some builder repositories (e.g. `heroku/builder`) don't publish a `latest` tag, so builders must be specified with a tag (e.g. `pack build --builder heroku/builder:24`). With exact string matching, a tagless `heroku/builder` entry in the known builders list wouldn't match these tagged references.

---

### Reproduction

##### Steps

1. `pack builder inspect paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest`

##### Current behavior

Output shows `Trusted: No`, because `IsKnownTrustedBuilder` performs an exact string match and `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest` does not equal `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base`.

##### Expected behavior

A known trusted builder listed without a tag should match any tag in that repository, consistent with the behavior for user-configured trusted builders.

##### Proposed solution

Make `IsTrustedBuilder` check both known and user-configured trusted builders using `name.ParseReference` for consistent reference matching: entries without a tag match any tag in the repository; entries with a tag require an exact match. This removes the need for callers to separately check `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()`.

Re: [#2266 (comment)](https://github.com/buildpacks/pack/pull/2266#issuecomment-2438351422) - a concern was raised that trusting by repository could implicitly trust unstable/RC tags (e.g. `builder:24-rc3.dev`). I verified via `crane ls` that all tagless known builders (the Paketo `paketobuildpacks/*` repositories) only publish semver version tags and `latest` - no RC, dev, alpha, beta, or pre-release tags exist in any of these repositories.

---

### Environment

Presumably all versions since PR #2266 was merged (which introduced this functionality).

comment.body:
I did a current-main code audit for this.

The current unit test already captures the failing behavior in `internal/builder/trusted_builder_test.go`: `IsKnownTrustedBuilder("paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base:latest")` and `:1.2.3` are expected to be false today.

Implementation-wise:

- `IsKnownTrustedBuilder()` is an exact string comparison over `KnownBuilders`.
- `IsTrustedBuilder()` does the reference-aware matching, but only for `cfg.TrustedBuilders`.
- Command callers then patch the two concepts together with `IsTrustedBuilder(...) || IsKnownTrustedBuilder(...)`, for example in `internal/commands/build.go`, `internal/commands/builder_inspect.go`, and `internal/commands/config_trusted_builder.go`.
- `pkg/client/build.go` defaults `opts.TrustBuilder` to `builder.IsKnownTrustedBuilder`, so fixing only the command layer would still leave the client default trust path using exact known-builder matching.

I think the smallest maintainable fix is to centralize the existing reference matching semantics and use that for both user-configured trusted builders and known trusted builders:

- tagless trusted entry matches by repository, as `IsTrustedBuilder()` already does for config entries;
- tagged trusted entry remains tag-exact, preserving `heroku/builder:24` vs `heroku/builder:26` behavior;
- known builders participate in the same helper, so command code and `pkg/client` default behavior cannot diverge.

Test surface I would expect:

- `internal/builder`: tagless known builder `paketobuildpacks/builder-jammy-base` matches `:latest` and another tag; explicit known builder `heroku/builder:24` does not match `heroku/builder:26`.
- caller coverage for at least `builder inspect` / `build` so the displayed `Trusted` and the build `TrustBuilder` option stay aligned.
- keep config trusted-builder tests for tagless and tagged user entries, since that is the behavior to reuse rather than replace.

Local check while auditing:

```bash
go test ./internal/builder -run TestTrustedBuilder -count=1
```

issue.title: Request support for viewing UTF-16
issue.body: some log file when i use moor to read it. It shows the content like showing in screenshot why? whereas same file when i use bat to read it show in proper format. I noticed if you used macos textEdit app and save the log in in rich text then moor shows the log like below screenshot most of time but bat tool render properly

comment.body:
I did a quick current-main audit with the attached `NTP.log` fixture.

What I see locally:

```text
$ file -I NTP.log
application/octet-stream; charset=binary

$ xxd -g 1 -l 32 NTP.log
31 00 35 00 34 00 38 00 34 00 34 00 20 00 30 00 38 00 3a 00 31 00 34 00 3a 00 33 00 32 00 2e 00

$ iconv -f utf-16le -t utf-8 NTP.log | sed -n '1,3p'
154844 08:14:32.2726347s - ---------- Log File Opened -----------------
154844 08:14:32.2731013s - RPC Call - Query Configuration
154844 08:14:32.2732153s - RPC Call - Query Provider Configuration
```

So this fixture is UTF-16LE without a BOM: ASCII bytes interleaved with NUL bytes. That also explains why simple charset detection reports `binary`.

The current code path looks byte-oriented all the way into rendering:

- `ZOpen()` / `ZReader()` only detect compression magic and otherwise return the raw stream.
- `reader.readStream()` splits input on byte `\n` and stores each line as raw bytes.
- `Line.Plain()` and `Line.HighlightedTokens()` convert `line.raw` directly with `string(line.raw)`.
- The text renderer then treats NULs / invalid text as unprintable characters, which matches the visual symptom.

I think a maintainable fix would be an input decoding layer after decompression and before line splitting, not a status-bar/rendering-only change. The scope decision is whether moor should support only explicit UTF-16 BOMs first, or also a conservative no-BOM heuristic for files like this one, for example a strong alternating-NUL pattern in the initial sample. A focused regression test could use a tiny UTF-16LE fixture and assert that the reader returns normal UTF-8 text from `Line.Plain()`.

issue.title: Bug: `--quit-if-one-screen` still sets alternate screen mode before exit when text fits into one screen
issue.body:
When using `moor` with the `--quit-if-one-screen` option, it still sets the alternate screen mode before exit when the text fits into one screen, causing the terminal to flash for a brief moment. Please see the below screen recording for a comparison between `moor` and `less`, both using the `--quit-if-one-screen` option:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/78365866-d9cf-4dff-9fb6-41fd2638a1bf

Watch for the scroll bar on the right edge of the recording if the switching between modes is hard to catch.

This is purely cosmetic though, and after reading the code I think it requires some structural change of how the pager gets run, so you may mark this issue as not very high priority.

Thank you as always!

comment.body:
I did a small repro and code-path check for this.

Repro command I used from a terminal-sized pty:

```bash
TERM=xterm-256color script -q /tmp/moor-425.typescript sh -c 'stty rows 24 cols 80; printf "hello\\n" | ./moor --quit-if-one-screen'
```

The captured output includes both alternate-screen enter and leave sequences before printing the final line back on the normal screen:

```text
ESC[?1049h ... ESC[?1049l ... hello
```

That matches what I see in the current flow: `pagerFromArgs()` creates the real screen as soon as the first byte is available, while `Pager.StartPaging()` only evaluates `QuitIfOneScreen` later inside the main loop after a redraw, once `ReadingDone` and `HighlightingDone` are true. At that point it sets `DeInit = false`, quits, closes the screen, and reprints the content after exit. So the final visible output is right, but the alternate screen has already been entered.

I think the tricky boundary is exactly the slow-stream case you mentioned: waiting for EOF before opening the UI would make `something_slow | moor --quit-if-one-screen` look hung until the producer closes. A narrow fix would probably need to be scoped to inputs where moor can know the complete content cheaply before screen setup, or to a very small startup grace period. I would not treat this as a simple pager-loop-only change because the decision currently happens after the real screen exists.

issue.title: Feature: adopt/claim workflow for CLI-bundled skills (~/.agents convention)
issue.body:
## Context

A growing set of tools now ship their own skills as part of install/setup — writing canonical files to `~/.agents/skills/<name>/` (the shared "agents" convention, tracked via `~/.agents/.skill-lock.json`) and placing symlinks in each detected AI agent's target dir (`~/.claude/skills/`, `~/.continue/skills/`, `~/.codex/skills/`, etc.).

**Confirmed examples in the wild:**

| Tool | Install | Ships |
|------|---------|-------|
| [`firecrawl/cli`](https://github.com/firecrawl/cli) | `npx -y firecrawl-cli@latest init` | 12 skills (sources: `firecrawl/cli`, `firecrawl/skills`) |
| [`googleworkspace/cli`](https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli) (`gws`) | `brew install googleworkspace-cli`, then `npx skills add https://github.com/googleworkspace/cli` | 100+ skills |
| [`obra/superpowers-marketplace`](https://github.com/obra/superpowers-marketplace) | Claude Code plugin marketplace | multi-skill bundle |
| [`backnotprop/plannotator`](https://github.com/backnotprop/plannotator) | Claude Code plugin marketplace | multi-skill bundle |

Different install mechanisms (npx postinstall, `npx skills add`, Claude Code plugins), same end state: canonical files in `~/.agents/skills/`, symlinks in agent target dirs, entries in `~/.agents/.skill-lock.json`.

## Conflict with skillshare

Skillshare's model: `~/.config/skillshare/skills/` is source of truth, target dirs are synced copies, "never edit targets directly." Bundled skills break this:

1. They write straight to `~/.agents/skills/` + agent target dirs, bypassing skillshare source
2. `skillshare audit` flags them as local/unmanaged
3. Adding `exclude: firecrawl*` silences audit but then only the subset of agents the installer detected gets the skills (firecrawl wrote to Claude + Continue only, missed Codex/Gemini/Pi which skillshare does manage)
4. If the user moves them into skillshare source manually, a future `<tool> setup skills` (or re-install) silently overwrites the target-dir symlinks, drifting from source

## Proposed: `skillshare adopt`

```bash
skillshare adopt "firecrawl*" --source-repos "firecrawl/cli,firecrawl/skills"
skillshare adopt "gws-*"      --source-repos "googleworkspace/cli"
```

Would:

1. Detect matching skills in `~/.agents/skills/` (or target dirs) not already in skillshare source
2. Move canonical files into `~/.config/skillshare/skills/`
3. Clean broken symlinks across **all** agent target dirs (including unmanaged ones like `~/.continue/skills/`)
4. Prune matching entries from `~/.agents/.skill-lock.json` by `source` / `sourceUrl` prefix
5. Run `skillshare sync`

## Questions

1. Does this fit inside #133's plugin-support scope, or warrant a separate lighter command focused on the `~/.agents/skills/` convention specifically?
2. Is there an idiomatic skillshare way to claim target-local skills today that I'm missing? `collect` looked close but I wasn't sure whether it handles the lock-file pruning piece or cross-agent symlink cleanup.
3. Would `skillshare doctor --suggest-adopt` (detecting these and offering to absorb them) be a lighter alternative to a dedicated command?

comment.body:
I did a current-main audit for the Codex/universal duplicate-root part of this issue.

What I found:

- `internal/config/targets.yaml` already models Codex primary global path as `~/.codex/skills` and `also_scans.global: ["~/.agents/skills"]`.
- `cmd/skillshare/doctor_shared_paths.go` already checks both same-target-path collisions and cross-target discovery overlaps.
- A local repro with `codex` + `universal` configured in global mode reports a `cross_target_discovery` warning in `doctor --json`: `codex (...) also scans .../.agents/skills ← universal`.
- `make test-unit` passes locally. Running raw `go test ./...` is not the right integration command because integration tests require `SKILLSHARE_TEST_BINARY`; the repo `scripts/test.sh` handles that by building `bin/skillshare` first.

Given that, I think the narrow next step for #135 is not a full `adopt` implementation yet. A small, maintainable PR could add actionable remediation suggestions to the existing doctor warnings, for example:

- text output: tell the user which target pair overlaps and suggest choosing one authoritative route;
- JSON output: expose a stable suggestion/action field so UI/automation can surface the same guidance.

That keeps collect/sync semantics and #133 plugin support out of scope while making the current diagnostic directly usable for the Codex + universal duplicate-root case.
